our model, this means that the content transitions to the empty state.
24


--- Page 25 ---
The simulation takes N = 1000 and λ = 0.1. The service rate µ varies by µ = λ × review ratio
with the review ratio ranging from 1% to 20%. This parameter, review ratio, captures the per-
centage of enqueued content that can be human reviewed on average. We restrict the review ratio
up to 20% to focus on an overloaded human review system motivated by the fact that signifi-
cantly more content is created than the amount humans can review on social media platforms. The
policy-violating views of an algorithm is averaged over 10 independent runs.
Baseline algorithms.
Each setting simulates a practical version of our OaRC algorithm (details
in Section 5.3) and three heuristic algorithms. All heuristics assign indices to waiting jobs and
review the jobs with the highest indices. The three index heuristics are:
• pViolating scheduling (pViolating): the index for content j in period t is Indexpv(j, t) =
pViolating(j); this heuristic has been deployed for content moderation at LinkedIn [Cha23].
• Velocity scheduling (Velocity):
the index for content j in period t is Indexve(j, t) =
pViolating(j)×View(j, d(j, t)−1), i.e., the probability of being policy-violating multiplied by
the number of views in the last period. This heuristic has been deployed at Meta [ABB+22].
• predicted IV (pIV) scheduling (pIV): the index of content j for period t is IndexpIV (j, t) =
pViolating(j)×predicted remaining views of content j from period t, i.e., a prediction for the
content’s IV (17). This heuristic was considered in [MSA+21], which documented a simulator
for Meta’s content moderation system. Different from the above two heuristics, pIV requires
an additional prediction model for future views, which we elaborate in Section 5.3.
One can view Velocity as a practical implementation of the generalized cµ-rule [VM95] as it
prioritizes content with largest increase in holding cost, approximated by the views this content got
in the last period. Similarly, pIV follows the same idea of the cµ/θ-rule [AGS10] by prioritizing
content with largest remaining holding cost. Both are canonical scheduling algorithms from the
literature handling heterogeneous job holding costs (as discussed in Section 1).
5.3
Practical implementation of our algorithm
The first step of implementing OaRC is identifying a state definition of content capturing its view
trajectory. We encode the state Sj(t) for a content j in period t by a six-dimensional vector:
Sj(t) =

pViolating(j), d(j, t), CumView(j, t −1), (View(j, d(j, t) −k))k∈{1,2,3}

,
(19)
corresponding to the probability of the content being policy-violating, the number of periods since
its creation, its total views before this period, and the per-period view in the last three periods. In
practice, the state can also incorporate static features of content (such as who created the content)
and dynamic features (such as number of likes and shares); see, e.g., [RXS+17, HKL+21] and the
references therein for the literature studying the prediction of content view trajectory.
With the state representation, the next step is to solve for γ⋆and the cost-to-go function
V f(γ⋆, ·). Fixing γ, the cost-to-go function V f(γ, ·) is solvable via standard value-function based
reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm such as deep Q-learning [MKS+15]. However, such an RL
algorithm is difficult to train in content moderation given the billions of pieces of new content being
created per day. Moreover, OaRC requires solving for γ⋆on top of solving the cost-to-go function.
25


--- Page 26 ---
We thus explore a hindsight approximation version of OaRC, which we call HOaRC and is
motivated by [SFC+23]. Recall from Section 3.1 that the cost-to-go function V f(γ, i) represents
the minimum expected future cost of a Markovian ski-rental problem, conditioning on an initial
state S1 = i. In its definition (3), the future cost trajectory is unknown through the uncertainty in
future states S2, . . . , SL where we recall from Section 2 the single-job Markov chain is a tree with at
most L levels. Now suppose instead that we do know the future state trajectory is Sk = sk, k ≤L
and thus the future cost trajectory is c(s2), . . . , c(sL). The hindsight minimum cost is equal to
min(γ, c(s2)+. . .+c(sL)) since the optimal action is to buy at a cost of γ if the total future renting
cost is higher than this buying cost, or the optimal action is to always rent. A hindsight cost-to-go
function is the expectation of the hindsight minimum cost, defined by
˜V (γ, i) = ES2,...,
"
min
 L
X
ℓ=2
c(Sℓ), γ
!  S1 = i, Sk+1 ∼P(Sk, ·), ∀k
#
.
Recall that when we instantiate our model for content moderation the cost c(Sj(t)) for a con-
tent j in period t corresponds to pViolating(j) × View(j, d(j, t)). Putting aside pViolating(j) and
assuming the cost for a content in a period is equal to its number of views in that period, the above
hindsight cost-to-go function ˜V leads to a simple offline training procedure. In particular, given the
training set Dtrain, for each content j let ˆs(j, τ) be the state representation of the view trajectory
of content j in the τ−th period and futureView(j, τ) be its future views after the τ−th period, i.e.,
futureView(j, τ) = PL
k=τ+1 View(j, k). We train a regression model for any given γ by
Mγ = Regression (ˆs(j, τ) →min {γ, futureView(j, τ)} : j ∈Dtrain, 1 ≤τ ≤L) ,
(20)
i.e., given the current state s of a content, the regressor outputs Mγ(s), which predicts the expec-
tation of the minimum between γ and the future number of views. In our implementation, we fit
an XGBoost regressor [CG16] with maximum depth equal to 10 and number of estimators equal
to 100.
When γ is set sufficiently large, Mγ predicts the future views of a content, which is what we use
for pIV scheduling in Section 5.2. For Algorithm 1, we need to set a suitable γ⋆. This can be done
by viewing γ⋆as a hyper-parameter to tune. In our simulation, we take γ⋆to be the 99%-percentile
of contents’ total views in the training set Dtrain.
Summarizing HOaRC, it takes as input a hyper-parameter γ⋆and train a regression model
M(γ⋆) according to (20). Then similar to (4), the index for content j in period t is given by
IndexHOaRC(j, t) = pViolating(j) × [View(j, d(j, t) −1) + Mγ⋆(ˆs(j, d(j, t)))] .
When γ⋆= 0, the index becomes the same as that of Velocity. Alternatively, if γ⋆= ∞, the index
behaves like pIV though it also considers the views in the last period. Tuning γ⋆seeks a balance
between the accurate instantaneous view information and the uncertain future view information.
5.4
Simulations on ads content with synthetic data
Our first setting focuses on content moderation of online ads. In social media platforms, advertisers
typically set up campaigns specifying a) a set of ads in the campaign, b) a metric they want to
optimize (such as click-through rate), c) budget to be spent on the campaign, and d) time duration
over which the campaign runs. From the set of ads in the campaign, the platform attempts to
26


--- Page 27 ---
identify the best performing ad(s) (as per the specified metric) and then spends most of the budget
on this set of ads. Ad platforms typically use exploration-exploitation techniques to find the best
ads [CKRU08, SBF17]. Hence, apriori, the view trajectory of an ad is uncertain. All ads tend to get
some views in the initial (exploration) period whereas later, only the set of chosen best performing
ads get most of the views. Further, after the exploration period, the views on the best performing
ads are typically stable over time due to pacing algorithms used by ad platforms [AGWY14].
Data generation.
Motivated by the above discussion, we create a synthetic dataset of ads as
follows. The training set and test set are generated in the same manner, but with different initial
random seed. For each dataset, there are 5, 000 campaigns. Each campaign has 5 ads. Denoting
the k−th ad of campaign u by ad (u, k), the training (test) dataset Dtrain (Dtest) consists of
5, 000 × 5 = 25, 000 contents where a content j is an ad (u, k) for some u ≤5, 000 and k ≤5.
The policy-violating probability pViolating((u, k)) is identical across all ads of the same campaign,
which is sampled from a Beta(1, 3) distribution. The actual violation Violating((u, k)) of an ad is
an i.i.d. Bernoulli random variable with mean pViolating((u, k)).
To generate the view trajectory of ads, a campaign u has a random variable Xu sampled from a
Pareto distribution with a shape parameter 0.8, which captures the campaign’s per-period budget.
An ad (u, k) has a mean reward (e.g. click-through rate) ru,k sampled from a Beta(1, 5) distribution.
Over a horizon of L = 100 periods, the platform seeks to identify an ad from this campaign with the
highest mean reward, which is a multi-armed bandit problem. We implement the UCB1 algorithm
[ACF02] for this purpose assuming that ad (u, k) has a Bernoulli reward with mean ru,k when
promoted (pulled) by the platform. The number of views ad (u, k) will get for the d−th period,
i.e., View((u, k), d) in the dataset (18), is equal to a Poisson random variable with rate Xu if this ad
is promoted for period d; and 0 otherwise. As illustrated in the first plot of Figure 3, the synthetic
view trajectories for ads with the highest number of views grow roughly linearly. This reflects the
characteristic of ad view trajectories where the ad identified as the best performing eventually gets
a stable number of views in every period due to the platform’s pacing algorithm.
Simulation results.
Recall that the review ratio r = µ/λ captures the average fraction of
enqueued contents that can be reviewed by human reviewers. Varying the review ratio r ∈R =
{0.01 + 0.005k: 0 ≤k < 40}, Figure 4 shows the effectiveness of HOaRC compared to the three
heuristics considered in Section 5.2.
Letting ViolatingViews(ALG, r) be the number of policy-
violating views for algorithm ALG when the review ratio is r, the left plot in Figure 4 plots the
percentage of reduced policy-violating views by using HOaRC, i.e., 1 −ViolatingViews(HOaRC,r)
ViolatingViews(ALG,r)
for
ALG ∈{pViolating, Velocity, pIV} and different review ratio r.
From the plot, HOaRC
consistently has fewer policy-violating views than the other heuristics across the range of review
ratios. Moreover, using HOaRC reduces the total policy-violating views under pViolating by
54% to 85%, and that under pIV or Velocity by 2.6% to 23.7%, depending on the review ratio.
To highlight how such prevalence reduction effort translates into reviewer-hour savings for a
platform, the right plot in Figure 4 shows the percentage of reviewer-hour savings by using HOaRC.
That is, for an algorithm ALG and a review ratio r, we find the minimum review ratio r′ from
the list R such that ViolatingViews(HOaRC, r′) ≤ViolatingViews(ALG, r) and then show 1 −r′
r .
This quantity captures the reduction in capacity (thus capturing reviewer-hour savings) to achieve
the same amount of policy-violating views when we switch from an algorithm ALG to HOaRC. In
the ads setting, HOaRC demonstrates 33% to 95% reviewer-hour savings compared to pViolating,
or 9% to 45% reviewer-hour savings compared to pIV and Velocity.
27


--- Page 28 ---

0.05
0.10
0.15
0.20
Review ratio

20
40

velocity

Figure 4: Ads: reduced policy-violating views (%) and reviewer-hour savings by HOaRC
5.5
Simulations on user-generated content with synthetic data
This section evaluates different scheduling algorithms on content moderation of UGC, i.e., content
generated and shared by social media users. The view patterns of UGC can be very different from
that of online ads for which the platform exhibits more control. To capture the unique nature
of UGC, this section generates synthetic view trajectory based on Hawkes processes, which have
been widely utilized to model view trajectories of UGC [RXS+17, HKL+21]. To further substantiate
findings in this sub-section, the next sub-section considers a real dataset from [RXS+17] on YouTube
videos.
Data generation.
For both the training and test datasets, we generate the view trajectory of
20, 000 pieces of content by a Hawkes process with exponentially decaying kernels motivated by
[HKL+21], which describes a prediction model for views of Facebook posts. In our synthetic dataset,
a content j has a hyper-parameter αj sampled uniformly from [0.8, 2] dictating the rate of decay
of its views. With one view in the first period, the number of views in the d−th period, View(j, d)
for 2 ≤d ≤200, is a Poisson random variable with mean given by
min
(
5000,
d−1
X
d′=1
␀1 + Yd′,d

View(j, d′)e−αj(d−d′)
)
,
(21)
where Yd′,d is a Pareto random variable with scale 4/αj, and we cap the value by 5000 to prevent
the view from blowing up. An intuitive understanding of (21) is that each view in a past period
d′ activates new views (e.g., produces new shares of the post) in period d with an exponentially
decaying probability. When an old view activates, the number of new views it prompts follows a
Pareto distribution. This is motivated by the observation that certain combinations of power-law
distributions approximate the node degree distribution of social networks [GKBM10]. See Figure 3
for examples of view trajectories in this synthetic dataset. Finally, the probability of policy-violating
pViolating(j) is sampled from a Beta(αj + 4/αj, 6) distribution.5
Simulation results.
As in Figure 4, Figure 5 show the percentage of reduced policy-violating
views and reviewer-hour savings when using HOaRC instead of existing heuristics. Other than
for a very small review ratio (r < 2%), HOaRC consistently outperforms existing heuristics with
5With the above data generation procedure, we allow the probability of a piece content being policy-violating to
be correlated with its view trajectory. This reflects the possibility that some inherent features of a content can impact
both its probability of being policy-violating and its number of views.
28


--- Page 29 ---
1.5% to 19% reduction in policy-violating views and 10% to 43% reduction in reviewer hours for
the synthetic UGC setting.
0.01
0.05
0.10
0.15
0.20
Review ratio
0
10
20
30
40
50
60
Reduced policy-violating views (%)
pIV
pViolating
velocity
0.01
0.05
0.10

Reviewer-hour savings (%)

pViolating

Figure 5: UGC: reduced policy-violating views (%) and reviewer-hour savings by HOaRC
5.6
Simulations on YouTube video data
The next setting we explore is content moderation for video content. Different from the previous
two sub-sections, which synthetically generate content view trajectory, we use the Active dataset
from [RXS+17] which contains daily number of views of certain YouTube videos.
Data description.
We provide a general discussion of the Active dataset, and refer the readers
to [RXS+17] for the detailed data collection process. The dataset contains 14, 041 YouTube videos.
Each video comes with a vector of daily number of views, the daily number of times it is tweeted
or shared, and video features such as the category and duration of this video. For the purpose
of our analysis, we only use the daily number of views. The dataset tracks the daily views for a
minimum of 119 days and a maximum of 237 days among these videos. The maximum number of
total views is 509, 245, 784 and the minimum is 18. See Figure 3 for examples of view trajectories
of these videos.
We generate a dataset of the form (18) for our simulation as follows. The dataset contains all
videos in the Active dataset and uses the daily number of views as View(j, t) for a video j and a
period t ≤L = 237. We fill the views by zero if the original dataset does not include enough days
of views for a video. The probability of policy-violating pViolating(j) is a uniform random variable
in [0, 1]. We equally and randomly separate the dataset into a training and a test set.
Simulation results.
Figure 6 shows our simulation results based on the Active dataset on
YouTube videos. HOaRC substantially outperforms pViolating and shows 3.2% to 8.5% reduc-
tion in policy-violating views or 7.8% to 25% reviewer-hour savings when compared to pIV or
Velocity and when the review ratio is at least 3%. The results are similar to what we observe in
the previous two synthetic simulations (Sections 5.4 and 5.5).
5.7
Robustness check: The impact of uncalibrated predictions
In Sections 5.4, 5.5 and 5.6, the ground truth violation of content j, Violating(j), is generated as a
Bernoulli random variable with mean pViolating(j). This assumes that the predicted probability

YouTube Video, Review ratio = 0.05

Figure 7: Policy-violating views when the maximum calibration error varies (5% review ratio)
of policy violation is well calibrated [DF83]. In practice, such an assumption often fails to hold true
due to non-stationarity in content trends; see the discussion in [ABB+22].
To study the robustness of our algorithm to uncalibrated predictions of policy-violating prob-
ability, we apply perturbed values of pViolating(j) when simulating the scheduling algorithms.
Specifically, the actual violation of content j, Violating(j) is still generated as a Bernoulli random
variable with mean pViolating(j). Then for a given maximum calibration error ε, which varies in
{0.03·k : 0 ≤k < 10}, the prediction for content j has a calibration error ej that is i.i.d. generated
from a uniform random variable [−ε, ε]. We simulate each scheduling algorithm with a perturbed
version of the policy-violating probabilities,
^
pViolating(j), which is equal to pViolating(j) + ej
clipped within [0, 1]. The review ratio is set as 5%.
Figure 7 shows the policy-violating views of the considered scheduling algorithms as a function
of the maximum calibration error ε for the same datasets considered in Sections 5.4, 5.5 and 5.6.
Note that we do not include pViolating as its policy-violating views is substantially higher than
the others. A few observations follow from the figure. First, the policy-violating views of any
algorithm generally increase with the maximum calibration error. Moreover, Velocity suffers the
most from increase in calibration error as compared to the other two algorithms, which is evident
in the synthetic UGC and YouTube video settings. Finally, the performance gain of our algorithm,
HOaRC remains consistent across different ε, supporting the robustness of HOaRC to calibration
error.
30


--- Page 31 ---
6
Conclusion
In this paper we consider the problem of scheduling with uncertain holding costs, which arises in
content moderation for social media platforms. Unlike existing approaches that either optimize
based on the instantaneous or the expected remaining holding cost, we design an algorithm that
adjusts to the opportunity of serving a job in the future once its uncertainty partly resolves. Our
algorithm is asymptotically optimal and outperforms existing approaches. On the analytical front,
our result relies on two key contributions: (a) a complete characterization of the fluid equilibrium
for any priority algorithm when the Markov chain forms a tree and (b) a new tail bound that
separates high-probability and almost-sure upper bounds on the drift of a Lyapunov function.
Our work opens up several interesting directions to further improve the operational efficiency
of content moderation in social media platforms:
• Our model assumes access to the Markov chain of jobs (that depends on the full view tra-
jectory of jobs). In practice, the future view trajectory of jobs that are removed from the
platform is unobservable. Designing a scheduling algorithm that can sufficiently learn the
view trajectories despite this censored feedback is an intriguing open direction.
• Our algorithm assumes perfect knowledge of the underlying Markov chain. On the other
hand, the instantaneous-cost based algorithmic principle (the cµ−rule) does not require such
information. Designing an index-based algorithm that achieves the Pareto frontier between
these two scenarios and adjusts to the potential estimation error is an interesting open ques-
tion. Questions of similar flavor have been studied in the learning-augmented literature with
respect to errors in cost-to-go functions [GM22, LLRW23] and scheduling [MD22, SGM22].
• Our model and algorithm require stationary arrival patterns and review capacity. The optimal
capacity dual γ⋆depends on both the arrival rate λ and the service rate µ. However, in
practice, the availability of human reviewers may fluctuate over time [MSA+21] and the
platform can have time-varying influx of content. This means that the capacity dual needs
to adapt to such changing patterns. Designing a scheduling algorithm that accounts for such
non-stationarity is a theoretically challenging and practically important open question.
Acknowledgements
This work was supported by an MIT-Meta Sponsored Research Agreement.

--- Page 40 ---
A
Tables of Notation
This section summarizes key notation used in the paper.
It contains three tables of notation,
classified by whether the notation is mainly for jobs’ tree-shaped Markov chain (Table 1), system
dynamics (Table 2), or the analysis (Table 3).
Table 1: Notation for jobs’ tree-shaped Markov chain
Symbol
Meaning
S
state space
L
number of levels of the tree
(maximum length of a job’s cost trajectory)
θ
minimum abandonment probability
S0, . . . , Sℓ, . . . , SL−1
states on level ℓof the tree
P(i, k)
the probability of transitioning from state i to state k
p(i)
the probability of transitioning from its parent to state i
c(i)
the instantaneous holding cost for a job with state i
r
the root of the tree
pa(i)
the parent of state i
ch(i)
the set of child(ren) of state i
Anc(i)
the set of ancestors of state i
Sub(i)
the set of states in the subtree of state i
pa(X), ch(X), Anc(X), Sub(X)
the union of parents, children, ancestors or subtrees
over all states in X
Top(X)
the top set of states in X, which is the minimum subset of X
whose subtree is a superset of X
Table 2: Notation for system dynamics
Symbol
Meaning
N
the system size which scales to ∞
λ, µ
the (normalized) arrival and service rates
R(t)
the random number of available servers in period t
Q(t) = (Qi(t))i∈S
the number of state-i jobs at the beginning of period t
R(t) = (Ri(t))i∈S
the number of served state-i jobs in period t
Z(t) = (Zi(t))i∈S
the number of remaining state-i jobs after service in period t,
which is equal to Qi(t) −Ri(t)
C(A), C(N, A)
the long-run average holding cost of an algorithm A
(when the system size is N)
Reg(A), Reg(N, A)
the regret of an algorithm A (when the system size is N)
40


--- Page 41 ---
Table 3: Notation for analysis
Symbol
Meaning
q = (qi)i∈S
fluid-scaled queue length for state i
ν = (νi)i∈S
fluid-scaled service for state i
C⋆
the minimum cost in the fluid LP (OriginalFluid)
P ⋆
the maximum prevented cost, which is λcf(r) −C⋆
π(i)
the probability of a job becoming state i,
which is Q
a∈Anc(i) p(pa(a), a)
π(i)/π(a)
the probability of a state-a job to become a state-i job
cf(a)
the conditional future cost for state i, P
i∈Sub(a) c(i)π(i)/π(a)
γ
capacity dual / price
β⋆(γ)
the optimal state duals when the capacity dual is γ
V f(γ, i)
the future cost-to-go function conditioning on price γ and state i
o = (o1, . . . , o|S|)
a priority ordering denoting the order in which states are served
o[h]
the first h states, {o1, . . . , oh} in the priority ordering o
m
the maximum position with P
i∈Top(o[m) λπ(i) ≤µ,
i.e., the service rate can serve (in expectation) all jobs in the top set of o[m]
p
the partially-served state which is om+1
unless if the above inequality is equality or m = |S|
κ
the degeneracy parameter defined in (10)
qo, νo
fluid equilibrium for priority algorithm based on ordering o
qOaRC, νOaRC
fluid equilibrium for OaRC
B
Additional Proofs
B.1
Proof of Lemma 3.1 (Section 3.2)
Fix a capacity dual γ ≥0. Recall that β⋆(γ) = (β⋆
i (γ))i∈S with β⋆
i (γ) = max(0, c(i)+V f(γ, i)−γ).
The following result establishes a connection between the cost-to-go function and β⋆(γ).
Lemma B.1. For any a ∈S, the cost-to-go function V (γ, a) = cf(a) −P
i∈Sub(a) β⋆
i (γ)π(i)/π(a).
Proof. For ease of notation, we omit the dependence on γ for β⋆(γ) and use β⋆= β⋆(γ) with
β⋆
i = β⋆
i (γ). Denoting the right-hand-side of the lemma by
˜V (a) = cf(a) −
X
i∈Sub(a)
β⋆
i π(i)/π(a) =
X
i∈Sub(a)
(c(i) −β⋆
i )π(i)/π(a),
we show by induction that ˜V (a) = V (γ, a) defined in (3). Recall that states in S are partitioned
into states S0, . . . , SL−1 in each level. Our induction moves from the largest level L−1 to the lowest
level 0. First, for states a ∈SL−1 in the highest level, Sub(a) = {a}. Then ˜V (a) = c(a) −β⋆
a =
min(c(a), γ) = V (γ, a) by (3). As our inductive hypothesis, suppose for some 0 < ℓ< L −1,we
41


--- Page 42 ---
have shown ˜V (i) = V (γ, i) for any i ∈Sℓ+1; our inductive step now establishes this for a ∈Sℓ.
˜V (a) =
X
i∈Sub(a)
(c(i) −β⋆
i )π(i)/π(a)
(i)
= c(a) −β⋆
a +
X
k∈ch(a)
X
i∈Sub(k)
(c(i) −β⋆
i )π(i)/π(k) · P(a, k)
(ii)
= c(a) −β⋆
a +
X
k∈ch(a)
P(a, k)V (γ, k)
(iii)
= c(a) −β⋆
a + V f(γ, a),
where (i) is because π(k) = π(a)P(a, k) for k ∈ch(a); (ii) is because of the induction hypothesis
and k ∈Sℓ+1; (iii) is by the definition of V f(γ, a) in (3). Using β⋆
a = max(0, c(i) + V f(γ, i) −γ)
gives ˜V (a) = min(γ, c(a) + V f(γ, a)) = V (γ, a), finishing the induction.
Proof of Lemma 3.1. Define the function f(β) = P
i∈S π(i)βi for a state dual vector β = (βi)i∈S),
which is the first part of D⋆(γ) in (Dual). Define the feasible set of state duals for D⋆(γ) by
B(γ) =


β ∈RS
≥0 : cf(a) −
X
i∈Sub(a)
βiπ(i)/π(a) ≤γ, ∀a ∈S


.
Then D⋆(γ) = µ · γ + λ minβ∈B(γ) f(β) by (Dual). The problem minβ∈B(γ) f(β) is an LP whose
dual is given by
max
y∈RS
≥0
X
a∈S
(cf(a) −γ)ya
s.t.
X
a∈Anc(i)
ya · π(i)
π(a) ≤π(i), ∀i.
(22)
We show the first result of the lemma (that β⋆(γ) is an optimal solution to D⋆(γ)) by proving
two properties: (1) it is feasible, i.e., β⋆(γ) ∈B(γ); (2) there exists y⋆(γ) that is feasible to (22)
such that f(β⋆(γ)) = P
a∈S(cf(a) −γ)ya and thus by weak duality β⋆(γ) is optimal.
To prove β⋆(γ) ∈B(γ), Lemma B.1 shows that cf(a) −P
i∈Sub(a) β⋆
i (γ)π(i)/π(a) is equal to
V (γ, a), which is upper bounded by γ by its definition (3). Therefore, β⋆(γ) ∈B(γ).
To show β⋆(γ) is optimal, we construct a feasible y⋆to (22) with f(β⋆(γ)) = P
a∈S(cf(a)−γ)y⋆
a.
This shows β⋆(γ) is optimal because by weak duality, any feasible β ∈B(γ) must have f(β) ≥
P
a∈S(cf(a) −γ)y⋆
a. We construct y⋆= (y⋆
a)a∈S as follows from the lowest level S0 to the highest
level SL−1, which ensures that for any state, at most one of its ancestor a has non-zero y⋆
a:
y⋆
a =
(
π(a), if c(a) + V f(γ, a) > γ and π(k) = 0, ∀k ∈Anc(a), k ̸= a
0, otherwise.
(23)
Since y⋆
i ≥0 and P
a∈Anc(i) yaπ(i)/π(a) ≤π(i) for any i, y⋆is feasible to (22). Moreover, by
rearranging terms, we verify that
X

42


--- Page 43 ---
If we establish Terms 1 and 2 are equal to zero, this implies P
i∈S π(i)β⋆
i (γ) = P
a∈S(cf(a) −γ)y⋆
a
and thus β⋆(γ) is optimal. We prove them as follows:
• For Term 1, Lemma B.1 shows cf(a) −P
i∈Sub(a) β⋆
i (γ)π(i)/π(a) = V (γ, a).
As a result,
Term 1 = P
a∈S y⋆
a(γ−V (γ, a)) = 0 because for any a, if y⋆
a > 0, (23) requires c(a)+V f(γ, a) >
γ and thus V (γ, a) = min(c(a) + V (γ, a), γ) = γ.
• For Term 2, if β⋆
i (γ) > 0, then c(i) + V f(γ, i) > γ. As a result, either y⋆
i = π(i) and any
ancestor a has y⋆
a = 0, or there exists an ancestor a with y⋆
a = π(a) by (23). This implies
π(i) −P
a∈Anc(i) y⋆
aπ(i)/π(a) = 0. As a result, Term 2 = 0.
Summarizing the above shows f(β⋆(γ)) = P
a∈S(cf(a) −γ)y⋆
a and thus β⋆(γ) is optimal to D⋆(γ).
For the second result of the lemma, since β⋆(γ) is an optimal solution,
D⋆(γ) = µ · γ + λf(β⋆(γ)) = µ · γ + λ
X
i∈S
π(i)β⋆
i (γ) = µ · γ + λ(cf(r) −V (γ, r)),
where, for the last equality, we use Lemma B.1.
B.2
Proof of Lemma 4.1 (Section 4.1)
Proof. Recall that the system state is (Q(t), R(t)) and initially Q(1) = 0. Define the system state
space by the set of states reachable from (0, n) for some n ∈[N]. To show irreducibility, it is
sufficient to show that there are paths connecting these states to state (0, 0) as state (0, 0) reaches
state (0, n) in one step for any n. Fix any state (q, n) in the system state space and suppose
Q(t) = q and R(t) = n. Since λ < 1, meaning that with non-zero probability there is no arrival
for this period. Moreover, given that the minimum abandonment probability θ > 0, every job in
the queue has non-zero probability to abandon. Moreover, R(t + 1) = 0 with non-zero probability.
As a result, Q(t + 1) = 0 and R(t + 1) = 0 with non-zero probability, which establishes that
the Markov chain is irreducible. For aperiodicity, since state (0, 0) can transition to itself in one
step, the Markov chain contains a self-loop, and is thus aperiodic. Finally, the Markov chain has
a finite state space because (i) Qi(t), R(t) are non-negative integers for any i; (ii) R(t) ≤N; (iii)
P
i∈S Qi(t) ≤PL−1
ℓ=0 A(t −ℓ−1) ≤L · N because a job with state i ∈Sℓin period t must have
arrived in period t −ℓ−1.
B.3
Proof of Lemma 4.2 (Section 4.1)
Proof. Since qo and νo satisfy (5), it suffices to verify the two constraints of (SimplerFluid), which
imply that (qo, νo) is feasible to (OriginalFluid). For the first constraint of (SimplerFluid), recall
from (6) that it is equivalent to having qo
i ≥νo
i for any state i. This inequality holds by definition
except for the partially-served state p (T-4), for which we verify that, if p ̸=⊥, then
qo
p −νo
p = λπ(p) −
µ −λ P
i∈Top(o[m]) π(i)
κ
=
λπ(p) −µ + λ P
i∈Top(o[m])\Sub(p) π(i)
κ
≥0,
where the second equality uses the definition of κ in (10). To see the last inequality, if p =⊥, then
κ = +∞and thus the fraction becomes zero. When p ̸=⊥, the partially-served state p is equal to
43


--- Page 44 ---
o[m+1] and Top(o[m+1]) = {p}∪Top(o[m])\Sub(p). If the last inequality is not true, then it contra-
dicts with the definition of m which defines m as the maximum position with P
i∈Top(o[m]) λπ(i) ≤µ.
For the second constraint of (SimplerFluid), we verify that P
i∈S νo
i = P
i∈Top(o[m]) λπ(i) ≤µ
when p =⊥, and when p ̸=⊥, it is
X
i∈S
νo
i =
X
i∈Top(o[m])\Sub(p)
λπ(i) + νo
p +
X
i∈Top(o[m])∩Sub(p)

λπ(i) −νo
pπ(i)
π(p) )

=
X
i∈Top(o[m])
λπ(i) + νo
p

1 −
X
i∈Top(o[m])∩Sub(p)
π(i)/π(p)


=
X
i∈Top(o[m])
λπ(i) + νo
pκ = µ,
where the second equality uses the definition of κ in (10) and the last equality uses the definition
of νo
p in (T-4). We have thus established that νo is feasible to (SimplerFluid).
B.4
Proof of Lemma 4.5 (Section 4.2)
Throughout this proof, we assume a fixed system size N and omit it in the notation. Fixing a
feasible algorithm ALG, we denote the number of state-i jobs in the queue at the beginning of
period t by Qi(t, ALG) and the number of served state-i jobs by Ri(t, ALG). To establish the lower
bound, for any horizon T, we define a feasible solution, qT = (qT

i ), to (OriginalFluid)

k∈Anc(i) P(pa(k), k).
Lemma B.2. The constructed (qT , νT ) is a feasible solution to (OriginalFluid).

B.5
Proof of Lemma 4.3 (Sections 4.2 and 4.3)
A result we need is that the probability of a job being in a top state of a set X is larger than that
of a subset Y. Although this is direct when Top(Y) ⊆Top(X), this condition is in general not true.
For example, consider X = {2, 3, 4, 5} and Y = {3, 5} in Figure 2. Then Top(X) = {2, 4} while
Top(Y) = {3, 5}. The proof of Lemma B.3 is based on induction argument.
Lemma B.3. If Y ⊆X ⊆S, then P
i∈Top(Y) π(i) ≤P
i∈Top(X) π(i).
46


--- Page 47 ---
Proof. Fix any X, Y ⊆S such that Y ⊆X. We denote the top set of Y by V = Top(Y). For any
state i, we define a function f(i) = π(i) −P
k∈Sub(i)∩V π(k), which is the difference between πi and
the sum of πk across any k that is in both the top set V and the subtree of i. Since Y ⊆X, its top
set V is in the subtree of Top(X), and thus has a partition {Vi}i∈Top(X) with Vi = V ∩Sub(i) for
i ∈Top(X) such that V = S
i∈Top(X) Vi. As a result,
X
i∈Top(X)
π(i) −
X
i∈Top(Y)
π(i) =
X
i∈Top(X)
π(i) −
X
i∈Top(X)
X
k∈Vi
π(k) =
X
i∈Top(X)
f(i).
(29)
To prove the lemma, it is sufficient to show f(i) ≥0 for any state i. Recall that states have a
partition based on their levels S = S0 ∪· · · SL−1. We show f(i) ≥0 by induction from SL−1 to S0:
• base case: for any i ∈SL−1, since it has no children, f(i) = π(i) −π(i)1 (i ∈V) ≥0.
• induction step: suppose f(k) ≥0 for any k ∈Sℓ+1. Given a state i ∈Sℓ, if i ∈V, this implies
there is no other state in its subtree inside V; otherwise V is not a top set. As a result,
f(i) = π(i) −π(i) = 0. If i ̸∈V,
f(i) = π(i) −
X
a∈ch(i)
X
k∈Sub(a)∩V
π(k) ≥π(i) −
X
a∈ch(i)
π(a) = π(i) −π(i)
X
a∈ch(i)
P(i, a) ≥0,
where the first inequality is by the induction hypothesis that f(a) = π(a)−P
k∈Sub(a)∩V π(k) ≥
0 since a ∈Sℓ+1.
The above shows that f(i) ≥0 for any state i. Using (29) gives P
i∈Top(X) π(i) ≥P
i∈Top(Y) π(i).
Proof of Lemma 4.3. We partition states into three classes SHi, SEq, SLo corresponding to states
with indices IndexOaRC(i) = c(i) + V f(γ⋆, i) higher, equal, or lower than γ⋆respectively. It is
sufficient to show that if a priority ordering o ranks these three classes in order (without ordering
within each), then its equilibrium is optimal to (OriginalFluid). This is because OaRC ranks all
states in a decreasing order of c(i) + V f(γ⋆, i), its corresponding priority ordering must rank SHi
before SEq before SLo.
For the remaining proof, suppose that a priority ordering o ranks SHi before SEq before SLo.
We show that νo is optimal to (SimplerFluid) using Lemma 4.6, which implies the optimality of
(qo, νo) to (OriginalFluid) by the equivalence between (OriginalFluid) and (SimplerFluid).
To do so, recall that the priority ordering o ranks states in SHi before states in SEq and those
in SEq before SLo.
Accordingly, there is a high position Hi and a low position Lo such that
c(oh) + V f(γ⋆, oh) > γ⋆for any h ≤Hi, c(oh) + V f(γ⋆, i) = γ⋆for any h ∈(Hi, Lo], and c(oh) +
V f(γ⋆, oh) < γ⋆for any h > Lo. Since Lemma 4.2 implies that νo is feasible to (SimplerFluid), it
remains to verify conditions (C-1) to (C-3) in Lemma 4.6 for νo. Before that, recall for the priority
ordering o, m is the maximum position such that P
i∈Top(o[m]) λπ(i) ≤µ. If the inequality is strict
and m < |S|, the partially-served state p is om+1; otherwise it is ⊥.
Condition (C-1) requires that P
i∈S νo
i = µ when γ⋆> 0. By Lemma 4.2, if the partially-
served state p ̸=⊥, then P
i∈S νo
i = µ. Thus we focus on the case when p =⊥. In this case,
P
i∈S νo
i = P
i∈Top(o[m]) λπ(i). Moreover, by our construction, the case p =⊥only happens when
P
i∈Top(o[m]) λπ(i) = µ, which implies P
i∈S νo
i = µ, or when m = |S| and P
i∈Top(o[m]) λπ(i) < µ.
For the latter scenario, note that when m = |S|, the top set Top(o[m]) = Top(S) is equal to the root
47


--- Page 48 ---
note r. As a result, in this scenario λπ(r) = λ < µ. Recall that γ⋆minimizes g(γ) := µ·γ −λV (γ, r)
among γ ≥0 by Lemma 3.1. Since now λ < µ, this implies
∀γ > 0, g(γ) = µ · γ −λV (γ, r) ≥µ · γ −λ · γ = (µ −λ)γ > 0 = g(0).
Therefore, γ⋆= 0 when λ < µ, satisfying (C-1). We thus verify this condition for νo.
Condition (C-2) requires qo
i = νo
i for any i ∈o[Hi]. To show this, it is sufficient to show that
P
i∈Top(o[Hi]) λπ(i) ≤µ as it implies any state i ∈o[Hi] is fully-blocked (T-2), partially-blocked (T-5)
or an empty state (T-3), which gives qo
i = νo
i by our construction. To show P
i∈Top(o[Hi]) λπ(i) ≤µ,
by (C-2) of Lemma 4.6 an optimal solution (q⋆, ν⋆) to (OriginalFluid) satisfies q⋆
i = ν⋆
i for i ∈o[Hi]
and thus
X
i∈Top(o[Hi])
λπ(i)
(5)
=
X
i∈Top(o[Hi])

q⋆
i +
X
a∈Anc(i)\{i}
ν⋆
a · π(i)
π(a)

Lemma 4.6
=
X
i∈Top(o[Hi])
X
a∈Anc(i)
ν⋆
a · π(i)
π(a)
=
X
a∈S
ν⋆
a


X
i∈Top(o[Hi])∩Sub(a)
π(i)

/π(a)
(∗)
≤
X
a∈S
ν⋆
aπ(a)/π(a) =
X
a∈S
ν⋆
a ≤µ,
where Inequality (*) applies Lemma B.3 with X = Sub(a) and Y = Top(o[Hi]) ∩Sub(a) and noting
that Top(X) = {a}, Top(Y) = Y, Y ⊆X. We thus show P
i∈Top(o[Hi]) λπ(i) ≤µ, which implies
Condition (C-2) as we showed at the beginning of this part.
Condition (C-3) requires νo
i = 0 for any i ̸∈o[Lo]. We show this by a case discussion:
• m + 1 ≤Lo: since our construction only allows state i ∈o[m+1] to have non-zero νo
i , this
gives νo
i = 0 for any i ̸∈o[Lo] since o[m+1] ⊆o[Lo] when m + 1 ≤Lo.
• m + 1 > Lo: By (C-3) of Lemma 4.6, an optimal solution (q⋆, ν⋆) to (OriginalFluid) satisfies
ν⋆
i = 0 for i ̸∈o[Lo] and thus
C⋆=
X
i∈S
c(i)(q⋆
i −ν⋆
i )
(7)
= λ
X
i∈S
π(i)c(i) −
X
a∈S
ν⋆
a
X
i∈Sub(a)
c(i)π(i)/π(a)
Lemma 4.6
=
λ
X
i∈S
π(i)c(i) −
X
a∈o[Lo]
ν⋆
a
X
i∈Sub(a)
c(i)π(i)/π(a)
= λ
X
i∈S
π(i)c(i) −
X
i∈Sub(o[Lo])
c(i)
X
a∈Anc(i)
ν⋆
aπ(i)/π(a)
≥λ
X
i∈S
π(i)c(i) −
X
i∈Sub(o[Lo])
λc(i)π(i) = λ
X
i̸=Sub(o[Lo])
π(i)c(i),
(30)
where the inequality is by the first constraint of (SimplerFluid). As a result,
X
i∈S
c(i)(qo
i −νo
i ) =
X
i∈S\Sub(o[Lo])
c(i)(qo
i −νo
i ) ≤λ
X
i∈S\Sub(o[Lo])
c(i)π(i) ≤C⋆,
where the first equality is because m + 1 > Lo and thus qo
i = νo
i for i ∈Sub(o[Lo]) by our
construction in (T-2), (T-3) and (T-5); the first inequality is because qo
i ≤λπ(i) for any i;
the last inequality is by (30). Hence, if m+1 > Lo, we directly show that (qo, νo) is optimal
to (OriginalFluid), the desired result of this lemma, which also implies (C-3) by Lemma 4.6.
48


--- Page 49 ---
Summarizing the above, Conditions (C-1), (C-2), (C-3) hold for νo. Lemma 4.6 thus shows it is
optimal to (SimplerFluid), implying that (qo, νo) is optimal to (OriginalFluid).
B.6
Proof of Lemma 4.6 (Section 4.3)
Proof. Recall that γ⋆is the optimal capacity dual and β⋆(γ⋆) is an optimal solution to (Dual) by
Lemma 3.1. To ease the notation, let β⋆
i = β⋆
i (γ⋆) = max(0, c(i) + V f(γ⋆, i) −γ) for any state i.
For any feasible service decision ν = (νi)i∈S to (SimplerFluid), the following equation holds true
by rearranging terms:
 
λ
X
i∈S
πiβ⋆
i + µγ⋆
!
−
X
a∈S
νacf(a) = γ⋆
 
µ −
X
i∈S
νi
!
|
{z
}
Term 1
+
X
i∈S
β⋆
i

λπ(i) −
X
a∈Anc(i)
νa
π(i)
π(a)

+
|
{z
}
Term 2
+
X
a∈S
νa

γ +
X
i∈Sub(a)
β⋆
i
π(i)
π(a) −cf(a)


|
{z
}
Term 3
.
By strong duality, ν is optimal to (SimplerFluid)if and only if the left hand side of the above
equation is equal to zero, which is equivalent to have Terms 1 to 3 equal to zero because each of
these terms is non-negative by the feasibility of ν to (SimplerFluid) and the feasibility of (γ⋆, β⋆)
to (Dual).
We prove the lemma by showing that Terms 1 to 3 are equivalent to Conditions (C-1) to (C-3):
• Term 1 and Condition (C-1) are equivalent because (i) if (C-1) is satisfied, then Term 1
evaluates to zero because γ⋆≥0; (ii) if Term 1 is zero, then either γ⋆or µ −P
i∈S νi is zero,
satisfying (C-1).
• Term 2 and Condition (C-2) are equivalent. If (C-2) is satisfied, it implies that for any state i
with β⋆
i > 0, we have qi, as defined in (5), equal to νi, implying that λπ(i) = P
a∈Anc(i) νa
π(i)
π(a).
Since β⋆
i ≥0 for any state i, this implies Term 2 evaluates to zero. In addition, if Term 2
is equal to zero, it requires that for any state i, either β⋆
i = 0 or λπ(i) = P
a∈Anc(i) νa
π(i)
π(a).
Therefore, if c(i) + V f(γ⋆, i) > γ, i.e., β⋆
i > 0, (5) requires qi = λπ(i) −P
a∈Anc(i)\{i} νa
π(i)
π(a) =
νi, satisfying ((C-2)).
• Term 3 and Condition ((C-3)) are equivalent. To see this, Lemma B.1 shows that V (γ⋆, a) =
cf(a) −P
i∈Sub(a) β⋆
i π(i)/π(a) and thus Term 3 simplifies to P
a∈S νa(γ −V (γ⋆, a)) where
V (γ⋆, a) = min(γ, c(i) + V f(γ⋆, i)). If (C-3) holds true, it implies that for any state a with
V (γ⋆, a) < γ, νa = 0 and thus Term 3 is equal to zero. Moreover, if Term 3 is equal to zero,
either νa = 0 or V (γ⋆, a) = γ for any state a, which implies (C-3).
Summarizing the above, ν is optimal to (SimplerFluid) if and only if Terms 1 to 3 are equal to
zero, which we established is equivalent to having Conditions (C-1) to (C-3).
B.7
Proof of Lemma 4.8 (Section 4.4)
Proof. The challenge in directly proving this result lies in the following two issues:
49


--- Page 50 ---
(a) the number of arrivals in each period is random;
(b) the correlation between jobs due to capacity constraint because a job’s presence in the queue
affects another job’s chance of getting service.
We circumvent these two issues by expanding the original system with fictitious jobs such that
(a) in each period there are always N new (fictitious) jobs and (b) even if a (fictitious) job leaves
because of service, we keep it in the system and thus remove correlation between jobs’ presence.
In particular, in each period there are always N new (fictitious) jobs. A new job j has probability
λ to have a state Sj(t + 1) = r, while with probability 1 −λ, the job has a state Sj(t + 1) =⊥,
i.e., it immediately abandons upon its arrival. The original set of new jobs, A(t), corresponds to
the fictitious jobs with Sj(t + 1) = r. Moreover, for the jobs that obtain service and leave, i.e., for
j ∈R(1) ∪· · · R(t), we assume that their states still transition according to the Markov chain. For
a period t, we define the set of fictitious job arrivals in this period by J (t), which deterministically
contains N jobs. The above expansion does not give more jobs than what exist in the original
system. Therefore, for a set of states X ⊆S, the number of waiting jobs with states in X is
X
i∈X
Qi(t) ≤
t−1
X
τ=1
X
j∈J (τ)
1 (Sj(t) ∈X) ≤
t−1
X
τ=t−min(L,|X|)
X
j∈J (τ)
1 (Sj(t) ∈X) ≤N min(L, |X|),
where the second inequality is because for a job j ∈J (τ) to have a state Sj(t) with level ℓ, it must
arrive in period t −ℓ, and the states in X contain at most min(L, |X|) different levels.
Since job states transition independently, X := Pt−1
τ=1
P
j∈J (τ) 1 (Sj(t) ∈X) is the sum of N(t−
1) independent binary random variables {Xj}j∈J (1)∪...∪J (t−1) where Xj = 1 (Sj(t) ∈X) . Moreover,
E [X] = E


t−1
X
τ=1
X
j∈J (τ)
1 (Sj(t) ∈X)

=
L−1
X
ℓ=0
X
i∈X∩Sℓ
X
j∈J (t−ℓ−1)
P{Sj(t) = i}
≤
L−1
X
ℓ=0
X
i∈X∩Sℓ
Nλπ(i) = Nλ
X
i∈X
π(i),
where the first equality is by partitioning states in X according to their levels and noting that only
jobs arriving in period t −ℓ−1 will have states of level ℓin period t; the inequality is because (i)
the size of J (t −ℓ−1) is N when t > ℓ+ 1 and equal to zero otherwise; (ii) the probability that a
new fictitious job transitions to state i ∈Sℓin ℓperiods is λπ(i). In addition,
X
i∈X
π(i) =
L−1
X
ℓ=0
X
i∈X∩Sℓ
π(i) ≤
L−1
X
ℓ=0
π(r) = L,
(31)
where the inequality applies Lemma B.3 to X ∩Sℓand S where Top(X ∩Sℓ) = X ∩Sℓand
Top(S) = {r}. Therefore, E [X] ≤NL. To give a high probability of P
i∈X Qi(t), consider two
cases. First, if E [X] = 0, it implies X = 0 since X ≥0 and thus P
i∈X Qi(t) = 0. Second, if
50


--- Page 51 ---
E [X] > 0, we finish the proof by
P
(X
i∈X
Qi(t) ≥Nλ
X
i∈X
π(i) + 3
√
NL ln 1
δ
)
≤P
(
X ≥Nλ
X
i∈X
π(i) + 3
√
NL ln 1
δ
)
≤P
(
X ≥E [X]
 
1 + 3
√
NL ln 1
δ
E [X]
!)
(a)
≤exp
 
−9NL ln2 1
δ
2E [X] + 2
√
NL ln 1
δ
!
(b)
≤exp
 
−9NL ln2 1
δ
4NL ln 1
δ
!
≤δ2,
where Inequality (a) is by noting X is the sum of independent binary random variables and applying
Fact 3; Inequality (b) is by E [X] ≤NL and the assumption that δ ∈(0, 1/e].
B.8
Proof of Lemma 4.9 (Section 4.4)
The proof follows a similar structure with that of theorem 1 in [BGT01]. We first establish the
following result.
Lemma B.4. Given the same conditions of Lemma 4.9 and that vwhp ≤vmax, then ∀a ≥B −vwhp,
P{Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp} ≤
vwhp
vwhp + ∆P{Φ(X(∞)) > a −vwhp} + 6εvmax
vwhp
.
Proof. Define a stochastic process {Y (t)} such that Y (t) = X(t + 1). Let ˆΦ(x) = max(a, Φ(x)).
Condition (iii) of the lemma also implies an almost sure upper bound on ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) −ˆΦ(X(t))
since if Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t))) ≤vmax holds almost surely, it implies almost surely that
ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) −ˆΦ(X(t)) = max(a, Φ(X(t + 1))) −max(a, Φ(X(t)))
≤max(a −a, Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t))) ≤vmax.
(32)
The assumption that E [Φ(X(∞))] < ∞implies E
h
ˆΦ(X(∞))
i
< ∞. Since X(t) has a limiting
distribution, both X(∞) and Y (∞) exist and are identically distributed (though not independent).
As a result, recalling the state space is X, the following equation holds:
E
h
ˆΦ(Y (∞)) −ˆΦ(X(∞))
i
= 0, and thus
X
x∈X
P{X(∞) = x} E
h
ˆΦ(Y (∞)) −ˆΦ(X(∞))
 X(∞) = x
i
|
{z
}
drift D(x)
= 0.
(33)
The proof proceeds by bounding the drift term D(x) considering three types of state x ∈G:
• Φ(x) ≤a −vwhp: in this case ˆΦ(x) = a. Moreover, by Condition (i) of the lemma, for any
period t, P{Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t) ≥vwhp|X(t) = x} ≤ε. That is, with probability at least
1 −ε, Φ(X(t + 1)) ≤Φ(x) + vwhp ≤a and ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) = a conditioning on X(t) = x.
Moreover, by (32), ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) −ˆΦ(X(t)) ≤vmax almost surely.
As a result, recalling
Y (t) = X(t+1), E
h
ˆΦ(Y (t)) −ˆΦ(X(t))
 X(t) = x
i
≤(1−ε)(a−a)+ε·vmax = εvmax. Since
this bound holds for any t, it is also true for the limiting distribution. We thus conclude the
drift D(x) ≤εvmax when Φ(x) ≤a −vwhp and x ∈G.
51


--- Page 52 ---
• Φ(x) > a+vwhp: since a ≥B−vwhp, Φ(x) ≥B. By Condition (ii) of the lemma, conditioning
on X(t) = x, with probability at least 1 −ε, Φ(X(t + 1)) ≤Φ(x) −∆, which gives
ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) −ˆΦ(X(t)) = max(a, Φ(X(t + 1))) −max(a, Φ(x))
= max(a, Φ(x) −∆) −Φ(x) ≤−∆,
where the last inequality is because Φ(x) ≥a + vwhp and vwhp ≥∆by assumption. By
(32), ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) −ˆΦ(X(t)) ≤vmax almost surely. Therefore,
E
h
ˆΦ(Y (t)) −ˆΦ(X(t))
 X(t) = x
i
≤(1−ε)(−∆)+ε·vmax = −∆+ε∆+εvmax ≤−∆+2εvmax,
where the last inequality is by the assumption that ∆≤vwhp ≤vmax. Taking t →∞shows
that the drift D(x) ≤−∆+ 2εvmax when Φ(x) ≥a + vwhp and x ∈G.
• Φ(x) ∈(a −vwhp, a + vwhp] : for this case, recall from (32) that ˆΦ(X(t + 1)) −ˆΦ(X)(t)) ≤
max(0, Φ(X(t+1))−Φ(X(t))). By Condition (i) of the lemma, conditioning on X(t) = x ∈G,
with probability at least 1−ε, the difference Φ(X(t+1))−Φ(X(t)) is at most vwhp. Therefore,
E
h
ˆΦ(Y (t)) −ˆΦ(X(t))
 X(t) = x
i
≤(1 −ε)vwhp + ε · vmax ≤vwhp + εvmax,
which implies D(x) ≤vwhp + εvmax when Φ(x) ∈(a −vwhp, a + vwhp] and x ∈G.
Applying the above three cases to (33) gives
0 ≤P {Φ(X(∞)) ≤a −vwhp} (εvmax) + P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp, X(∞) ∈G} (−∆+ 2εvmax)
+ P {Φ(X(∞)) ∈(a −vwhp, a + vwhp]} (vwhp + εvmax) + P {X(∞) ̸∈G} vmax.
Since P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp, X(∞) ∈G} ≥P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp} −P{X(∞) ̸∈G} by the
union bound and P{X(∞) ̸∈G} ≤ε by the lemma condition, the above inequality simplifies to
0 ≤−∆P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp} + vwhpP {Φ(X(∞)) ∈(a −vwhp, a + vwhp]} + 6εvmax
= −∆P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp} + vwhp (P {Φ(X(∞)) > a −vwhp} −P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp})
+ 6εvmax.
Therefore, by rearranging terms and recalling the assumption that vwhp ≥1, we obtain
P {Φ(X(∞)) > a + vwhp} ≤
vwhp
vwhp + ∆P {Φ(X(∞)) > a −vwhp} + 6εvmax
vwhp
.
Proof of Lemma 4.9. Without loss of generality, we assume vwhp ≤vmax in the proof; otherwise the
result of the lemma is implied by the case with vwhp = vmax. For any ℓ≥0, applying Lemma B.4
52


--- Page 53 ---
iteratively with a = B + 2vwhpℓ−vwhp, B + 2vwhp(ℓ−1) −vwhp, . . . , B −vwhp gives
P{Φ(X(∞)) > B + 2vwhpℓ} ≤
vwhp
vwhp + ∆P{Φ(X(∞)) > B + 2vwhp(ℓ−1)} + 6εvmax
vwhp
≤


vwhp + ∆
2
P{Φ(X(∞)) > B + 2vwhp(ℓ−2)} +

vwhp
.
B.9
Proof of Lemma 4.10 (Section 4.4)
The proof requires an additional lemma that lower bounds the number of jobs that leave the
system without getting service in each period due to abandonment. Specifically, for a state i, let
Fi(t) = Zi(t) −P
k∈ch(i) Qk(t + 1) be the number of state-i jobs transitioning into the ⊥state at
the end of period t (abandoning). Given a minimum abandonment probability θ > 0 and a set of
states X, the below concentration result shows that P
i∈X Fi(t) is approximately lower bounded by
θ P
i∈X Zi(t).
Lemma B.5. For any set of states X, period t and δ ∈(0, e−1],
P
(X
i∈X
Fi(t) ≥θ
X
i∈X
Zi(t) −
r
NL ln 1
δ
)
≥1 −δ2.
Proof. Letting θi = 1 −P
k∈ch(i) P(i, k) be the abandonment probability for a state-i job, Fi(t)
is a Binomial distribution with Zi(t) trials and success probability θi.
Moreover, Fi(t), i ∈X,
are independent because each job transitions according to the Markov chain independently. As a
result, the sum P
i∈X Fi(t) is the sum of P
i∈X Zi(t) independent random variables taking value in
{0, 1}. Conditioning on P
i∈X Zi(t) = n, the conditional expectation of P
i∈X Fi(t) is at least nθ.
Applying Hoeffding’s Inequality (Fact 2) gives
P
(X
i∈X
Fi(t) ≥nθ −
r
n ln 1
δ

X
i∈X
Zi(t) = n
)
≥1 −exp
 
−2n ln 1
δ
n
!
= 1 −δ2.
(34)
53


--- Page 54 ---
As P
i∈X Zi(t) ≤P
i∈X Qi(t) ≤NL almost surely by Lemma 4.8, the law of total probability shows
P
(X
i∈X
Fi(t) ≥θ
X
i∈X
Zi(t) −
r
NL ln 1
δ
)
=
NL
X
n=0
P
(X
i∈X
Fi(t) ≥θ
X
i∈X
Zi(t) −
r
NL ln 1
δ

X
i∈X
Zi(t) = n
)
P
(X
i∈X
Zi(t) = n
)
≥
NL
X
n=0
P
(X
i∈X
Fi(t) ≥nθ −
r
n ln 1
δ

X
i∈X
Zi(t) = n
)
P
(X
i∈X
Zi(t) = n
)
≥(1 −δ2)
NL
X
n=0
P
(X
i∈X
Zi(t) = n
)
= 1 −δ2,
where the first inequality is because n ≤NL, the second inequality is by (34), and the last one is
because P
i≤X Zi(t) ≤NL almost surely.
Proof of Lemma 4.10. Recall that T = Top(o[m]) and thus Sub(T ) = Sub(o[m]). We define the
following good event G of system states such that if X(t) = (Q(t), R(t), Q(t + 1)) ∈G, then
(G-1) P
i∈T Qi(t + 1) ≤λN P
i∈T π(i) + 3
√
NL ln 1
δ;
(G-2) P
i∈Sub(T ) fi(X(t)) ≥θ P
i∈Sub(T ) zi(X(t))−
q
NL ln 1
δ, where we denote the number of aban-
doning state-i jobs by fi(X(t)) = zi(X(t)) −P
k∈ch(i) Qk(t).
We note that P{X(∞) ∈G} ≥1 −2δ2. To see this, X(∞) satisfies (G-1) with probability at least
1 −δ2 by Lemma 4.8 and it satisfies (G-2) with probability 1 −δ2 by Lemma B.5. Using a union
bound gives P{X(∞) ∈G} ≥1 −2δ2. We thus showed (i) of the lemma.
To show (ii), for any t, condition on X(t) and define an event Gservice where the number of
available services is not far below its mean:
Gservice =
(
R(t + 1) ≥µ · N −
r
N ln 1
δ
)
.
(35)
By Hoeffding’s Inequality (Fact 2), P{Gservice|X(t)} ≥1−δ2 since R(t+1) is a Binomial distribution
with mean Nµ and is independent from X(t).
Given X(t) ∈G and Gservice, we show (14) in Lemma 4.10 by expanding the drift:
Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) =
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t)
=
X
i∈Sub(T )
(Qi(t + 1) −Ri(t + 1)) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t)
=
X
i∈T
Qi(t + 1) +
X
i∈Sub(T )\T
Qi(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Ri(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t).
(36)
By the rule of Prio(o) in (8), the R(t) available services must first serve jobs with states in Sub(T )
before they are used to serve jobs in S \ Sub(T ). As a result, there are two cases:
54


--- Page 55 ---
• P
i∈Sub(T ) Ri(t + 1) = P
i∈Sub(T ) Qi(t + 1): in this case the available services are sufficient to
serve all jobs in Sub(T ). The first three terms in (36) cancel out, showing that in this case
Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) ≤−
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t).
• P
i∈Sub(T ) Ri(t + 1) < P
i∈Sub(T ) Qi(t + 1): in this case P
i∈Sub(T ) Ri(t + 1) = R(t + 1). This
is because the jobs with states in Sub(T ) must take all R(t) available services (otherwise we
can increase the Ri(t + 1) for some i ∈Sub(T )). Putting this into (36) gives
Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) ≤
X
i∈T
Qi(t + 1) +
X
i∈Sub(T )\T
Qi(t + 1) −R(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t)
(37)
Recall the definition of m that λ P
i∈T π(i) ≤µ. Combining this bound with Property (G-1)
of event G and the definition of event Gservice shows
X
i∈T
Qi(t + 1) −R(t + 1) ≤Nλ
X
i∈T
π(i) −Nµ + 3
√
NL ln 1
δ +
r
N ln 1
δ ≤4
√
NL ln 1
δ .
(38)
Putting (38) into (37) shows
Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) ≤
X
i∈Sub(T )\T
Qi(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t) + 4
√
NL ln 1
δ .
To upper bound the right hand side, the set of parents of Sub(T ) \ T is a subset of Sub(T ).
Therefore,
Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) ≤
X
i∈Sub(T )\T
Qi(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t) + 4
√
NL ln 1
δ
=
X
i∈Sub(T )
X
k∈ch(i)
Qk(t + 1) −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t) + 4
√
NL ln 1
δ
(a)
= −
X
i∈Sub(T )
Fi(t) + 4

δ
(b)
≤−θ
X
i∈Sub(T )
Zi(t) + 5
√
NL ln 1
δ = −θΦ(X(t)) + 5
√
NL ln 1
δ .
where (a) is by the definition of the number of abandoning state-i jobs Fi(t) and (b) is by
Property (G-2) of event G.
Summarizing the above two cases shows that if X(t) ∈G and Gservice happen, then (14) holds true.
Lastly, since the parents of all states in Sub(T )\T must be in Sub(T ), P
i∈Sub(T )\T Qi(t+1) ≤
P
i∈Sub(T ) Zi(t).
As a result, Φh(X(t + 1)) −Φh(X(t)) ≤P
i∈T Qf(t + 1) ≤NL by (36) and
Lemma 4.8. Therefore, the drift has an almost sure upper bound NL.
55

B.10
Proof of Lemma 4.7 (Section 4.4)
Proof of Lemma 4.7. The proof is by applying Lemmas 4.9 and 4.10. Lemma 4.10 shows that there
exists a good event G with P{X(∞) ∈G} ≥1 −2δ2, such that conditioning on X(t) ∈G, (14)
holds with probability at least 1 −δ2. Take ε = 2δ2, ∆= vwhp = 5
√
NL ln 1
δ, B = 2∆
θ , vmax = NL.
Conditions of Lemma 4.9 are met because P{X(∞) ∈G} ≥1 −ε, E [Φ(X(∞))] < +∞, and
(i) if X(t) ∈G, with probability at least 1 −δ2 ≤1 −ε conditioning on X(t), (14) holds and
implies that Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) ≤5
√
NL ln 1
δ = vwhp;
(ii) if X(t) ∈G and Φ(X(t)) ≥B, with probability at least 1 −δ2 ≥1 −ε conditioning on X(t),
(14) gives Φ(X(t + 1)) −Φ(X(t)) ≤−θΦ(X(t)) + 5
√
NL ln 1
δ ≤−θ · B + ∆= −∆.
(iii) Φh(X(t + 1)) −Φh(X(t)) ≤vmax = NL by the last result of Lemma 4.10.
As a result, Lemma 4.9 applies. Taking ℓ= ⌊3 ln 1
δ⌋(thus ℓ+ 1 ≥2 ln 1
δ) in Lemma 4.9 gives
P

Φh(X(∞)) > B + 6vwhp ln 1
δ

≤2−(ℓ+1) + 6(ℓ+ 1)εvmax ≤δ2 + 6

3 ln 1
δ + 1

2δ2NL
≤(1 + 6 · (3 + 1) · 2)δ2NL ln 1
δ = 49NLδ2 ln 1
δ ,
where we use the assumption that δ ≤e−1. Moreover,
B + 6vwhp ln 1
δ ≤
2
θ + 6 ln 1
δ

5
√
NL ln 1
δ ≤40θ−1√
NL ln2 1
δ ,
which finishes the proof.
B.11
Proof of Lemma 4.11 (Section 4.4)
Recall the degeneracy parameter κ = 1−P
i∈Top(o[m])∩Sub(p) π(i)/π(p) in (10). We first lower bound
it by the minimum abandonment probability θ.
Lemma B.6. The minimum abandonment probability θ lower bounds the degeneracy parameter κ.
Proof. Recall that Tm = Top(o[m]) and thus κ = 1 −P
i∈Tm∩Sub(p) π(i)/π(p) from (10). Showing
κ ≥θ is equivalent to show (1 −θ)π(p) ≥P
i∈Tm∩Sub(p) π(i).
To see why this is true, define the set X = Sub(ch(p)), which is the subtree of children of p. Then
Top(X) consists of the children of p, i.e., Top(X) = ch(p). In addition, define Y = Tm ∩Sub(p).
The top set of Y is itself since it is a subset of Tm, which is itself a top set of o[m]. As a result,
X
i∈Tm∩Sub(p)
π(i) =
X
i∈Y
π(i) =
X
i∈Top(Y)
π(i)
(a)
≤
X
i∈Top(X)
π(i) =
X
i∈ch(p)
π(i)
(b)
≤(1 −θ)π(p),
where inequality (a) is because Y is a subset of X and Lemma B.3 applies; inequality (b) is because
P
i∈ch(p) π(i) = P
i∈ch(p) π(p)P(p, i) = π(p) P
i∈ch(p) P(p, i) ≤π(p)(1 −θ) as θ is the minimum
abandonment probability. We thus finish the proof by establishing an equivalence of κ ≥θ.
56


--- Page 57 ---

is equal to om+1. The limiting distribution Zp(∞) satisfies Zp(∞) = Qp(∞) −Rp(∞) with
Rp(∞) = min

(39)
Define a good event Gz with the following three properties:
(Gz-1) the available service is not far below its mean, i.e., R(∞) ≥Nµ −
√
N ln N;
(Gz-2) the number of jobs with states in Tm \ Sub(p) is not high, i.e.,
X
i∈Tm\Sub(p)∪{p}

X

NL ln N.
(Gz-3) there are few jobs with states in Sub(Tm) \ Tm, i.e., P
i∈Sub(Tm)\Tm Qi(∞) ≤˜U
√
N.
Each of the three properties happens with probability at least 1 −1/N: the first is by Hoeffding’s
Inequality in Fact 2; the second is by Lemma 4.8; and the third is by the lemma assumption and
the fact that P
i∈Sub(o[m]) Zi(∞) stochastically dominates P
i∈Sub(Tm)\Tm Qi(∞). Applying a union
bound gives P{Gz} ≥1 −3/N. Conditioning on Gz, (39) gives
Zp(∞) ≤max

i∈Tm π(i) ≤µ, which contradicts the definition that m is the maximum such

For any state i in the subtree of Sub(p), its steady-state number of waiting jobs must satisfy
E [Qi(∞)] ≤E [Zp(∞)] π(i)
π(p) because (i) to be in state i a job must first be in state p; (ii) the condi-
tional probability of a state-p job becoming a state-i job is π(i)/π(p) assuming the job does not leave

because of getting service. As a result, E

The last equation is because Zi(t) converges in distribution to Zi(∞) and is bounded (0 ≤Zi(t) ≤
Qi(t) and Qi(t) ≤N by Lemma 4.8). Therefore, C(N, Prio(o)) −P
i∈S zo

.
The proof bounds each of the three terms. For the first term, since zo
i ≥0 by definition, it suffices
to bound P
i∈Sub(o[m]) E [Zi(∞)] . Note that P
i∈Sub(o[m]) E [Zi(∞)] ≤P
i∈Sub(o[m]) E [Qi(∞)] ≤NL.
Hence, by Lemma 4.8 and the assumption that P{P
i∈o[m] Zi(∞) ≤˜U
√
N} ≥(N −1)/N:

√

(41)
58


--- Page 59 ---
For the second term, an induction on the tree shows that for any state k and i with i ∈Sub(k), the
number of remaining jobs with state i in period t is at most E [Zi(t)] ≤E [Zk(t −d(k, i))] π(i)/π(k),
where d(k, i) is the tree distance from state k to state i. As a result, E [Zi(∞)] ≤E [Zk(∞)] π(i)/π(k)
by taking t →∞. Moreover, as discussed in the proof sketch, any state i ∈Sub(p)Sub(o[m]) is either
the partially-served state (T-4) or a partially-reduced state (T-6). This implies zo
i = zo
pπ(i)/π(p)
by our construction. Using this observation, the second term satisfies
X
i∈Sub(p)\Sub(o[m])
ciE [Zi(∞) −Nzo
i ] =
X
i∈Sub(p)\Sub(o[m])
ci
␀E [Zi(∞)] −Nzo




= (E [Zp(∞)] −Nzo
p)

where (a) uses that P

inequality is by Lemma B.3 and (b) uses Lemma 4.11.
For the third term, observe that for any state i and period t, E [Zi(t)] ≤Nλπ(i) by induction
from its parent state.
Therefore, E [Zi(∞)] ≤Nλπ(i) by taking t →∞. Since any state i ∈
S \Sub(o[m])\Sub(p) is an un-reduced state (T-1) with zo
i = λπ(i), the third term is equal to zero.
Combining the above bounds for the three terms gives

B.13
Proof of Lemma 4.4 (Sections 4.2 and 4.4)
To give the proof of Lemma 4.4, we first simplify the bound in Lemma 4.7 by tuning the parameter δ.
Lemma B.7. With probability at least 1−1/N, the number of remaining jobs in Sub(o[m]) is upper
bounded by 160θ−1√
NL ln2(55NL).
Proof. Lemma 4.7 shows that for any δ ∈(0, e−1], P{P
i∈Sub(o[m] Zi(∞) > 40θ−1√
NL ln2 1
δ} ≤
48NLδ2 ln 1
δ. To highlight the dependence on δ, we define
U(δ) = 40θ−1√
L ln2 1
δ ,
ε(δ) = 49NLδ2 ln 1
δ .
(42)
We aim to pick a δ⋆∈(0, e−1] such that ε(δ⋆) ≤1/N and then bound U(δ⋆). Lemma 4.7 then
implies P{P
i∈Sub(o[m]) Zi(∞) > U(δ⋆)
√
N} ≤1/N.
Set δ⋆to be the maximum value in (0, 1] such that

ln(49NL) + ln ln 1
δ⋆

= ln 1
δ⋆.
(43)
59


--- Page 60 ---
Such a δ⋆must exist because (1) both the left and right hand sides of the equation are continuous
function when δ ∈(0, 1]; (2) when δ →0+, the right hand side grows to infinity faster than the left
hand side; (3) when δ = 1, the left hand side is larger than zero but the right hand side is zero.
Moreover, δ⋆≤e−1 as otherwise the left hand side of (43) is larger than the right hand side.
We first show ε(δ⋆) ≤1/N. Taking exponentiation of both sides of (43) gives
49NL ln 1
δ⋆= 1
δ⋆⇒ε(δ⋆) = δ⋆⇒ε(δ⋆) =
1
49NL ln 1
δ
⋆≤1
N .
Upper bounding U(δ⋆) is less immediate as it involves a term ln2 1
δ⋆. We define two auxiliary
quantities, δ0 = 1/ exp(2 ln(49NL)) and δ1 = 1/ exp(exp(4)) and consider three cases:
• if δ⋆≥δ0, then ln 1
δ⋆≤2 ln(49NL).
• if δ⋆≥δ1, then ln ln 1
δ⋆≤ln ln 1
δ1 and (43) gives ln 1
δ⋆≤ln(49NL) + 4 ≤2 ln(55NL).
• It is impossible to see δ⋆< min(δ0, δ1). This is because if δ⋆< δ1, then ln 1
δ⋆> exp(4) and
thus ln ln 1
δ⋆≤1
2 ln 1
δ⋆by Fact 1. As a result,
ln(49NL) + ln ln 1
δ⋆≤ln(49NL) + 1
2 ln 1
δ⋆
(a)
< 1
2 ln 1
δ⋆+ 1
2 ln 1
δ⋆= ln 1
δ⋆,
which contradicts (43). The strict inequality (a) is by the case assumption that δ⋆< δ0.
Summarizing the above discussion gives ln 1
δ⋆≤2 ln(55NL) and thus using (42),
U(δ⋆) ≤160θ−1√
L ln2(55NL),
which finishes the proof.
Proof of Lemma 4.4. By Lemma B.7,
P



X
i∈Sub(o[m])
Zi(∞) ≤160θ−1√
NL ln2(55NL)


≥1 −1/N.
As a result of Lemma 4.12,
C(N, Prio(o)) −N
X
i∈S
cizo
i ≤10cmaxLθ−1(
√
NL ln N + 160θ−1√
NL ln2(55NL))
≤1610cmaxθ−2L1.5 ln2(55NL)
√
N.
B.14
Alternative regret bound (Remark 1)
We outline how the analysis in Section 4 gives a regret bound of OaRC,
Reg(N, OaRC) ≤O(θ−3.5 ln2(NL)
√
N),
60


--- Page 61 ---
which only logarithmically depends on the maximum level L. From the proof of Theorem 1 in Sec-
tion 4.2, it suffices to show how Lemma 4.4 also applies for some U′(L, θ, ln N) = O(θ−3.5 ln2(NL)).
We do so by showing that all the dependence on L can be replaced by 1/θ in Lemmas 4.7 to 4.12,
except the 48NLδ2 ln 1
δ term in Lemma 4.7 and the almost sure upper bound N(min(L, |X|)) in
Lemma 4.8. These exceptions are what leads to the ln(L) dependence in the revised regret bound;
how to further remove this dependence would require a more refined version of our tail bound in
Lemma 4.9 that does not need an almost sure upper bound on the drift.
A new result we need is the following upper bound.
Lemma B.8. The sum P
i∈S π(i) is at most 1
θ.
Proof. Recall that states partition into levels S = S0 ∪. . . ∪SL−1. We show by induction that
P
i∈Sℓπ(i) ≤(1 −θ)−ℓfor any ℓ. When ℓ= 0, S0 = {r} and thus P
i∈S0 π(i) = π(r) = 1. For ℓ> 0,
X
i∈Sℓ
π(i) =
X
i∈Sℓ−1
X
k∈ch(i)
π(k) =
X
i∈Sℓ−1
π(i)
X
k∈ch(i)
P(i, k) ≤
X
i∈Sℓ−1
π(i)(1 −θ) ≤(1 −θ)ℓ.
As a result, P
i∈S π(i) = PL−1
ℓ=0
P
i∈Sℓπ(i) ≤PL−1
ℓ=0 (1 −θ)ℓ≤1
θ.
Changes to the proofs are as follows:
• The revised proof of Lemma 4.7 in Appendix B.10 will select ∆= vwhp = 5
p
N/θ ln 1
δ;
• The proof of Lemma 4.8 in Appendix B.7 replaces the bound in (31) with Lemma B.8 and
changes L to 1/θ from then on.
• The revised proof of Lemma 4.10 in Appendix B.9 requires several changes. For Lemma B.5,
we can show that P{P
i∈X Fi(t) ≥θ P
i∈X Zi(t) −
p
N/θ ln 1
δ} ≥1 −O(δ2). To show this, we
replace the almost sure upper bound of P
i∈X Zi(t) ≤NL below (34) by a high probability
upper bound that P{P
i∈X Zi(t) ≤2Nλ/θ ln 1
δ)} ≥1 −O(δ2) using the revised Lemma 4.8.
The derivation below (34) follows by replacing L with O(ln 1
δ/θ) and separating out the
event that P
i∈X Zi(t) > 2Nλ/θ ln 1
δ), which happens with small probability. The proof of
Lemma 4.10 follows by replacing the L in (G-1) with 1/θ and the L in (G-2) with 2/θ ln 1
δ and
updating the proof accordingly. These changes allow the term
√
NL in Lemma 4.10 becomes
p
N/θ with suitably changed constants.
• The revised proof of Lemma 4.11 in Appendix B.11 changes the
√
NL term in (Gz-2) to
p
N/θ.
• The revised proof of Lemma 4.12 replaces the L in (41) with 1/θ, which is valid because
P
i∈Sub(o[m]) E [Qi(∞)] ≤Nλ P
i∈S π(i) ≤N/θ by Lemma B.8. Similarly, the equality (a)
below (41) is valid with L replaced by 1/θ because the same proof of Lemma B.8 shows
P
i∈Sub(p) π(i) ≤π(p)/θ.
• The revised proof of Lemma 4.4 replaced the
√
NL term in Lemma B.7 with
p
N/θ.
61


--- Page 62 ---
C
Useful Facts
This section states several useful analytical facts and concentration bounds.
The below fact shows condition for x such that a ln x ≤x/2.
Fact 1. If a ≥1 and x ≥exp(4a ln(e · a)), then a ln x ≤x/2.
Proof. Let g(x) = x/2 −a ln x. Its derivative is g′(x) = 1/2 −a/x which is positive when x ≥2a.
Moreover, by Taylor’s expansion,
g(e4a ln(e·a)) = 1
2e4a ln(e·a) −4a2 ln(e · a) ≥1
2 + 2a ln(e · a) + 4a2 ln2(e · a) −4a2 ln(e · a) ≥0,
where the last inequality is because 4a2 ln2(e · a) ≥4a2 ln(e · a). Using e4a ln(e·a) ≥4a ln(e · a) ≥2a
shows that the function g(x) is increasing and non-negative over x ≥exp(4a ln(e · a)).
We use Hoeffding’s Inequality, whose proof can be found in, e.g., Theorem 2.8 of [BLM13].
Fact 2 (Hoeffding’s Inequality). Given n independent random variables X1, . . . , Xn such that Xi ∈
[ai, bi] almost surely for all i. Letting S = Pn
i=1(Xi −E [Xi]), for any x > 0,
max (P{S ≤−x}, P{S ≥x}) ≤exp

−
2x2
Pn
i=1(bi −ai)2

.
We also use the following Chernoff bound restated from theorem 2.4 of [CL06].
Fact 3 (Chernoff bound). Given n independent random variables X1, . . . , Xn such that Xi ∈{0, 1}
for all i. Letting X = Pn
i=1 Xi, for any ε > 0,
Pr{X ≥E [X] + ε} ≤exp

−
ε2
2(E [X] + ε/3)

.
62
